points: Figures that have the same shape and size are congruent; they can be superimposed exactly one over the other, and may be rotated or flipped before superimposing.
To recreate a figure exactly, sufficient measurements must be taken — for example, two arm lengths alone do not fix a shape, but two arm lengths plus the included angle do.
For two circles to be congruent their radii must be equal; for two rectangles, both length and breadth must respectively match.

Five conditions guarantee congruence: SSS (all three sides equal), SAS (two sides and the included angle equal), ASA (two angles and the included side equal), AAS (two angles and a non-included side equal), and RHS (right angle, hypotenuse, and one leg equal in right-angled triangles).

SSA (two sides and a non-included angle) does NOT guarantee congruence — two different triangles can share the same SSA measurements; AAA (three equal angles) also does NOT guarantee congruence as it produces similar but not necessarily congruent triangles.
When writing ΔABC ≅ ΔXYZ, the vertex order specifies the correspondence — A↔X, B↔Y, C↔Z — and all corresponding sides and angles are automatically equal.

In any triangle, angles opposite to equal sides are equal; in an isosceles triangle (two equal sides), the two base angles are equal — proved using the RHS congruence of the two halves formed by the altitude.
In an equilateral triangle all three sides are equal, so by the isosceles property all three angles are also equal; since they sum to 180°, each angle is exactly 60°.
Congruence is a powerful tool for deducing geometric properties — the angle properties of isosceles and equilateral triangles are formally proved using congruence conditions, not just observed.
